beats.activity: Wake up, tea, light stretching and journaling at home before the rain starts.
beats.reasoning: Maya is an early riser who loves quiet mornings; after yesterday's high energy, she needs grounding. Journaling and tea fit her mindful routine. Starting at 6:15am gives her calm time before the day accelerates.

beats.activity: Walk through Fremont neighborhood to Gas Works Park for sunrise and reflection despite incoming rain.
beats.reasoning: Yesterday's diary mentions Gas Works Park as a cherished spot; Maya returns for morning clarity. Drizzle won't stop a nature-loving early riser, and the walk from Fremont is short and familiar.

beats.activity: Return home, shower, prepare for work and grab breakfast.
beats.reasoning: Rain is steady by now; Maya heads home to get ready. She needs to be at Capitol Hill studio by 9am, so this is her prep window.

beats.activity: Commute from Fremont to Capitol Hill studio; take I-5 south (SR-520 eastbound closure doesn't affect westbound commute, but adds 10 min buffer for rain/traffic).
beats.reasoning: SR-520 eastbound is closed 9am–noon, but Maya travels westbound into Capitol Hill, so the closure has minimal direct impact. Rain and wet roads warrant extra time. She arrives 20 min before her first class.

beats.activity: Teach morning vinyasa flow class; energize students on a grey Tuesday.
beats.reasoning: Maya's first scheduled class. Grey, drizzly weather often brings people indoors to yoga for warmth and grounding—studio will likely have decent attendance despite the rain.

beats.activity: Break between classes: hydrate, light snack, check in with studio staff.
beats.reasoning: Short rest window; Maya avoids screens (her habit) and stays at the studio. Rain continues outside, so no walking errands.

beats.activity: Teach power yoga class; lead students through challenging flows.
beats.reasoning: Second class of the day. Rain outside reinforces the cozy, grounded atmosphere inside. Maya's calm presence helps students settle into their practice on a dreary morning.

beats.activity: Lunch break: grab a smoothie bowl at a nearby Capitol Hill café, avoid screens.
beats.reasoning: Maya loves smoothie bowls and avoids screens before noon. Rain is tapering by noon. Quick break at a neighborhood spot keeps her grounded before the afternoon shift.

beats.activity: Teach midday power class; guide students through strength and balance work.; Finish studio admin, tidy teaching space, reflect on the day's classes.
beats.reasoning: Third class; rain has mostly cleared by early afternoon. Continuation of yesterday's pattern of back-to-back teaching, though today's energy feels calmer (low 35/100 yesterday evening carries into today).

beats.activity: Commute home from Capitol Hill to Fremont; take I-5 north, enjoy the clearing afternoon.
beats.reasoning: Rain has cleared; northbound I-5 is unaffected by the SR-520 closure. Maya heads home with enough daylight left to decompress. Travel time ~40 min in post-rain traffic.

beats.activity: At home: stretch, shower, make a simple dinner, journal about the day.
beats.reasoning: Maya arrives home with low residual energy; she spends the afternoon recovering with yoga, self-care, and her journaling practice. No evening plans—she's content to be still and reflect.

beats.activity: Sleep; rest and recover after a full teaching day.
beats.reasoning: Early bedtime (5:30pm start) reflects Maya's low energy carryover and her natural early-riser rhythm. 12.5 hours of sleep helps her recover for tomorrow. Sleep beat ends at 6am, visible overnight span.

diary:
Woke early to that soft grey light filtering through my Fremont apartment. Made tea, journaled a bit, then couldn't resist walking to Gas Works Park despite the drizzle—something about rainy mornings makes the city feel like it's just for you. Got soaked but it was worth it.

Teaching days always blur together, but today felt good. The morning vinyasa class had this collective energy despite the weather keeping people indoors. My power class at noon was tough—lots of students pushing through, which meant I had to show up fully for them. Grabbed a smoothie bowl at Solstice between classes and actually sat with my thoughts instead of scrolling.

The drive home felt lighter once the rain cleared. I was tired but that good kind of tired—the kind where your body reminds you it did real work. Made pasta, stretched on the living room floor, and watched the sky turn that soft blue-grey it does in early summer. Ready for sleep.
thought_process: I started the day wanting to honor the quiet before the rush—tea and journaling felt essential, especially knowing I'd be "on" for six hours of teaching. The walk to Gas Works was impulsive but necessary; rainy mornings make me reflective, and I needed that grounding before managing other people's energy. The SR-520 closure didn't touch my route, but I built in buffer time anyway because I've learned rain turns Seattle drivers unpredictable. Teaching through grey weather requires intention—I could feel students' sluggishness, so I leaned into energizing sequences. Skipping screens at lunch was deliberate; I was craving actual rest, not digital distraction. By afternoon, my body was asking for gentleness, so the commute home felt like permission to downshift. The clearing weather matched my mood shifting from "on" to "done."
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
